content.text:
{He makes me lie down in green pastures. He leads me beside still waters.}{Psalm 23:2}
 {Perpendicularity is the soul of geometry.}{— Blaise Pascal}
Orthogonal sets provide clean, independent coordinate systems.

content.title: Orthogonal and Orthonormal Sets
content.content:
A set $\{\vect{u}_1, \ldots, \vect{u}_p\}$ is:

- **Orthogonal** if $\vect{u}_i \cdot \vect{u}_j = 0$ 
 for all $i \neq j$.

- **Orthonormal** if orthogonal and 
 every vector has norm 1.

content.title: Orthogonal Sets are Independent
content.content: An orthogonal set of nonzero vectors is linearly independent.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Coordinates in an Orthogonal Basis
content.content:
If $\{\vect{u}_1, \ldots, \vect{u}_n\}$ is an orthogonal basis for $W$ 
and $\vect{y} \in W$, then:
\[
\vect{y} = \frac{\vect{y} \cdot \vect{u}_1}{\vect{u}_1 \cdot \vect{u}_1}\vect{u}_1 + 
\cdots + \frac{\vect{y} \cdot \vect{u}_n}{\vect{u}_n \cdot \vect{u}_n}\vect{u}_n
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Orthogonal Matrix
content.content:
A square matrix $Q$ is **orthogonal** 
if $Q\transpose Q = I$, i.e., $Q\inv = Q\transpose$.

content.title: Properties of Orthogonal Matrices
content.content:
$Q$ is orthogonal iff its columns form an orthonormal set. Equivalently:

1. $\norm{Q\vect{x}} = \norm{\vect{x}}$ for all $\vect{x}$ (preserves length)

2. $(Q\vect{x}) \cdot (Q\vect{y}) = \vect{x} \cdot \vect{y}$ (preserves dot product)

3. $\det(Q) = \pm 1$
content.label: Theorem

marginNotes.text:
But all things should be done 
decently and in order.
